subtopic: COP30 Health, Oceans, and Methane Initiatives
title: COP30 Emergency Actions and Key Outcomes
focus: Early warning systems, climate-health financing, heat resilience, and food waste reduction initiatives launched at COP30.
rationale: This section covers distinct funds and coalitions (SOFF, CHFC, Cool Coalition) that are highly testable for UPSC prelims regarding their parent bodies and primary targets.
sectionExcerpt: Emergency Action includes the 2030 Strategy for Climate Risk and Early Warning Systems (CREWS) and Brazil's Call to Action on Integrated Fire Management. Other outcomes include the Systematic Observation Impact Bond by SOFF (UNEP, WMO, UNDP) to help LDCs and SIDS meet GBON standards. The Climate and Health Funders Coalition committed $300 million. The Global Mutirao Against Extreme Heat, led by the Cool Coalition, aims to reduce heat risks for 3 billion people, alongside the Global Cooling Pledge. The Food Waste Breakthrough, led by UNEP, targets halving global food waste and reducing methane emissions by 7% by 2030.
evidenceBullets: Systematic Observation Impact Bond is the world's first impact bond on weather and climate data, created by SOFF (UNEP, WMO, UNDP).
Climate and Health Funders Coalition (CHFC) committed an initial $300 million focusing on extreme heat, air pollution, and infectious diseases.
Global Mutirao Against Extreme Heat is led by the COP30 Presidency and UNEP-led Cool Coalition, joined by 185 cities.
Food Waste Breakthrough is led by UNEP and targets halving global food waste and a 7% reduction in methane emissions by 2030.

subtopic: COP30 Health, Oceans, and Methane Initiatives
title: COP30 Sectoral Initiatives: Oceans, Methane, and Energy
focus: Various global coalitions and toolkits launched at COP30 targeting ocean conservation, methane reduction, agriculture, and clean energy.
rationale: Contains multiple specific initiatives (Oceans Breakthroughs, NOW, Global Carbon Harvest Coalition, UNEZA, Super Pollutant Accelerator) with clear targets and partner organizations, ideal for match-the-following questions.
sectionExcerpt: Key initiatives at COP30 include the Oceans Breakthroughs Dashboard to track progress in 5 ocean sectors and the Global network of regenerative seascapes aiming to mobilize USD 20 billion. The No Organic Waste (NOW) initiative, supported by the Global Methane Hub, commits to cutting 30% of methane from organic waste by 2030. The Global Carbon Harvest Coalition involves Brazil, India, and Kenya focusing on soil organic carbon and biochar. The Utilities for Net Zero Alliance (UNEZA) aims to invest USD 1 trillion by 2030. The Super Pollutant Country Action Accelerator by CCAC targets reductions in methane, black carbon, HFCs, and tropospheric ozone.
evidenceBullets: Oceans Breakthroughs Dashboard tracks 5 ocean sectors to help cut emissions by up to 35% by 2050.
No Organic Waste (NOW) initiative is supported by the Global Methane Hub and targets a 30% cut in methane from organic waste by 2030.
Global Carbon Harvest Coalition is a joint effort by Brazil, India, and Kenya focusing on soil organic carbon, biochar, and enhanced rock weathering.
Utilities for Net Zero Alliance (UNEZA) aims to invest approximately USD 1 trillion by 2030 to expand clean energy.
Super Pollutant Country Action Accelerator is a CCAC programme targeting methane, black carbon, HFCs, and tropospheric ozone.

subtopic: COP30 Health, Oceans, and Methane Initiatives
title: Country or Regional Platforms for Climate and Nature Finance
focus: The announcement by India and other developing nations to create climate finance platforms supported by the Green Climate Fund.
rationale: A specific, India-involved climate finance mechanism supported by a major international fund (GCF), making it highly relevant for UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: At COP30, India, along with 12 other developing countries and the African Islands States Climate Commission (AISCC), announced plans to develop 'Country or Regional Platforms for Climate and Nature Finance'. These are strategic, country-driven mechanisms translating climate priorities into programmatic investment approaches. They are supported under the Green Climate Fund's (GCF) Readiness Programme, which is the largest climate action capacity-building facility for developing countries. GCF already supports the Brazil Country Platform and the Caribbean Regional Platform.
evidenceBullets: India, 12 developing countries, and the AISCC announced plans for Country or Regional Platforms for Climate and Nature Finance at COP30.
The platforms are supported by the Green Climate Fund's (GCF) Readiness Programme.
The GCF Readiness Programme is the largest climate action capacity-building facility for developing countries.
Existing platforms supported by GCF include the Brazil Country Platform and the Caribbean Regional Platform.
